hints.estate: 'Estate' is not a Latin form — the correct imperative of 'esse' is 'estote'
hints.estis: 'Estis' means 'you are' (indicative) and does not appear in this sentence

hints.to eat: That would be 'edere' — 'Estote' is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to stand: That would be 'stare' — 'Estote' is from 'esse' (to be)
hints.to remain: That would be 'manere' — 'Estote' is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be 'estis' — 'Estote' is imperative
hints.present imperative (plural): Present imperative of 'esse' would be 'este' — 'Estote' with -tote is the future imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sitis' — 'Estote' is future imperative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat)
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.sisto, sistere, stiti, statum: These are the parts of 'sistere' (to cause to stand)

steps.target: perfect passive participle (used as predicate adjective)
choices: perfect passive participle (used as predicate adjective)
steps.reveal: parati = prepared / ready (perf. pass. part., nom. masc. pl.)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'parantes' — 'parati' is perfect passive
hints.future active participle: Future active would be 'paraturi' — 'parati' is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'parandi' — 'parati' is perfect passive participle

hints.pareo, parere, parui, paritum: These are the parts of 'parere' (to obey)
hints.pario, parere, peperi, partum: These are the parts of 'parere' (to give birth/produce)
hints.comparo, comparare, comparavi, comparatu: These are the parts of 'comparare' (to prepare/compare) — a compound of 'parare'

hints.genitive: Genitive singular would be 'parati', but context with 'Estote' (pl.) shows nom. pl.
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'paratis' — 'parati' is nominative plural
hints.vocative: Vocative plural would also be 'parati', but here it is predicate nominative with 'Estote'

steps.prompt: The future imperative 'estote' is rare in Latin. It implies?

hints.an immediate command: An immediate command would use present imperative — future imperative implies ongoing obligation
hints.a polite request: Politeness is not the primary function — the future imperative stresses permanence
hints.a past command: Commands cannot be in the past — 'estote' is future imperative, stressing permanence
